originalTitle: Zohran Mamdani plans ‘major address’ to mark US’s 250th anniversary ahead of Trump’s Mount Rushmore speech – live
summary: The city’s immigrant mayor will deliver his remarks ‘surrounded by recently naturalized citizens’, according to his office
content: A federal ⁠judge blocked a proposed restriction on mail-in voting across the US, challenging a crackdown on elections ordered by Donald Trump. Judge Emmet Sullivan of the US district court for the District of Columbia ruled that a US Postal Service (USPS) plan to deny ballots to voters in states that do not turn over their voter rolls to the federal government should not proceed. It was the second time in recent weeks that the US president’s plan to restrict mail-in-voting has suffered a setback in court. The decision by Sullivan bars the postal service from enforcing an executive order issued by Trump in March that called for sweeping changes to the administration of elections nationwide. In accordance with the order, the postal service issued a proposed rule on 2 June that would ‌require states to give the US Department of Homeland Security and other agencies access to lists of voters and to adopt new balloting procedures before the mail agency would make deliveries. If states did not comply, USPS would refuse to deliver the ballots. Sullivan, who was appointed ⁠to the bench by the Democratic former president Bill Clinton, sided with the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP) civil rights group, which argued that the new rule would run ‌afoul of a 2021 legal settlement which forced USPS officials to take “extraordinary measures” to ensure timely delivery of ballot mail. Colorado’s Democratic governor, Jared Polis, has fired two members of his clemency board, the New York Times reports, after they denounced his decision to commute the prison sentence of Tina Peters, a county official who was convicted of tampering with voting machines in an effort to prove the conspiracy theory that Donald Trump won the 2020 election. The two board members, Hannah Seigel Proff and Azra Taslimi, revealed in a Denver Post opinion article last month the clemency review board had voted unanimously to deny a request for commutation from Peters. “Peters recounted events ‘through her eyes’ and sought to justify conduct that twelve jurors had deemed criminal,” they wrote. “Clemency is not an accelerated appeal. It is not a pressure release valve for politically inconvenient sentences. It exists for the people the legal system cannot reach, not for the people who simply prefer not to wait for it. Using it this way, for this person, at this moment, doesn’t just dishonor the process. It degrades the remedy for everyone else who is waiting their turn at the only door they have left,” the two women, both former public defenders, added. They added that Peters was unrepentant and used her freedom to continue to falsely claim election fraud had denied Trump victory in a state he lost by nearly half a million votes in 2020. In the op-ed, they wrote: Within hours of her release, Peters appeared on a podcast and immediately resumed her attacks on the integrity of U.S. elections. On Steve Bannon’s podcast, she repeated the debunked conspiracy theory that voting machines cheated Donald Trump out of reelection in 2020 and portrayed herself as a martyr to the effort to expose it. She called her release a miracle. She expressed no contrition. She offered no acknowledgment of the harm her conduct caused to the clerks who were threatened, to the election workers whose lives were upended, to the democratic institutions she exploited from a position of public trust. The governor said he was moved by her admission that she made a mistake. She walked out of prison and told the world she made no mistake at all. The governor’s review board normally operates in secret, but the two members argued that staying silent was not an option in this case. “Azra Taslimi and I chose to come forward and co-author this piece for The Denver Post because we believe silence equals complicity”, Proff wrote on LinkedIn. “We thought about staying silent, but silence isn’t neutral, its a choice and it has a cost,” Taslimi wrote on the same platform. “Injustice doesn’t usually need permission to keep happening. It just needs everyone in the room to stay quiet. For people to decide it’s not their fight. And the thing just keeps going, unchallenged, because silence is so much easier than being the one who points at it.” On Tuesday, Trump posted a photograph of Peters with him in the Oval Office on social media, along with a raft of false claims about her and the 2020 election. The mayor of New York, Zohran Mamdani, plans to deliver what his office calls a “major address” on Friday to mark the 250th anniversary of the United States, “reflecting on New York City’s role in our national history and its position as the nation’s symbolic gateway.” Mamdani is scheduled to speak at 10am local time, meaning that his remarks will come hours before Donald Trump marks the anniversary in a speech at Mount Rushmore. The city’s immigrant mayor will deliver his remarks “surrounded by recently naturalized citizens,” from a desk used by George Washington in 1789, when he was inaugurated as the country’s first president in New York. “This desk is older than the Resolute Desk and was used by Washington when it was located in Federal Hall, the first capitol building for the United States,” Mamdani’s aides point out, noting that the desk currently used in the Oval Office lacks the same connection to the country’s original, New York-based administration. Mamdani’s remarks on the country’s founding are likely to differ widely from those of the president, if they are at all influenced by his father Mahmood Mamdani’s book, Neither Settler Nor Native, which argues that the modern nation-state began in 1492 with “the taking of overseas colonies in the Americas by the same Castilian monarchy that spearheaded ethnic cleansing.” The first chapter of the book is about the ethnic cleansing of North America by European settlers and the role of slavery in the formation of the United States. “This is a book about the United States as a founding experience in modern
chineseTitle: 佐兰·马姆达尼 (Zohran Mamdani) 计划在特朗普拉什莫尔山演讲之前发表“重要讲话”，纪念美国建国 250 周年
chineseSummary: 据他的办公室称，该市的移民市长将在“最近入籍的公民的簇拥下”发表讲话
chineseContent: 一名联邦法官阻止了美国各地邮寄投票限制的提议，对唐纳德·特朗普下令镇压选举提出质疑。美国哥伦比亚特区地区法院法官埃米特·沙利文裁定，美国邮政局 (USPS) 拒绝向不将选民名册移交给联邦政府的各州选民投票的计划不应继续进行。这是美国总统限制邮寄投票的计划近几周来第二次在法庭上遭遇挫折。沙利文的决定禁止邮政部门执行特朗普三月份发布的一项行政命令，该命令要求对全国选举管理进行彻底改革。根据该命令，邮政服务于 6 月 2 日发布了一项拟议规则，要求各州允许美国国土安全部和其他机构获取选民名单，并在邮件机构投递之前采用新的投票程序。如果各州不遵守规定，美国邮政局将拒绝交付选票。沙利文被民主党前总统比尔·克林顿任命为替补席，他支持全国有色人种协进会 (NAACP) 民权组织，该组织认为，新规则将与 2021 年的法律解决方案相冲突，该解决方案迫使美国邮政局官员采取“非常措施”，以确保及时送达选票邮件。据《纽约时报》报道，科罗拉多州民主党州长贾里德·波利斯解雇了其赦免委员会的两名成员，此前他们谴责了他为县官员蒂娜·彼得斯减刑的决定，蒂娜·彼得斯因篡改投票机而被定罪，以证明唐纳德·特朗普赢得 2020 年大选的阴谋论。两位董事会成员，
whyItMatters: 这条信息属于全球新闻信号，适合用于观察外部环境、政策变化、地缘风险和市场情绪。
